sections.heading: 1. What is a Sealed Class?
sections.content: A sealed class restricts its inheritance hierarchy. All subclasses must be defined in the same package (typically the same file). It behaves like an advanced enum but allows each subclass to hold its own state.

sections.heading: Core Idea
sections.content: Unlike enum constants which are single instances, sealed class subclasses can have multiple instances and can store different data.

sections.heading: 2. Sealed Class vs Enum
sections.content: Sealed classes are more flexible than enums and are commonly used to represent complex states such as API responses or UI states.

sections.heading: Key Differences
items: Enum: Each constant is exactly one instance.
Sealed Class: Subclasses can be data classes with different state.
Enum: Best for fixed values like Days or Colors.
Sealed Class: Best for complex states like API Results or UI States.

sections.heading: 3. Real-World Android Example: UI State
sections.content: Sealed classes are commonly used in Android to represent UI states such as Loading, Success, and Error.

sections.heading: Defining a Result Sealed Class
sections.content: Each subclass represents a different possible state of the UI.

sections.heading: Handling States with when
sections.content: The Kotlin compiler knows all subclasses of a sealed class. When using when as an expression, it forces you to handle all possible cases (exhaustive check).

sections.heading: Exhaustive when Example
sections.content: Each possible state is handled explicitly without needing an else branch.

sections.heading: 4. Sealed Interfaces
sections.content: Sealed interfaces work similarly to sealed classes but allow multiple inheritance. Use them when you do not need shared implementation or constructor logic.

sections.heading: Sealed Interface Example
sections.content: Classes implementing a sealed interface must follow the same restricted hierarchy rules.
sections.code:
sealed interface NetworkEvent

class ClickEvent(val id: Int) : NetworkEvent
class SwipeEvent(val direction: String) : NetworkEvent

sections.heading: 5. Benefits in Clean Architecture
sections.content: Sealed classes improve type safety and clarity in large applications, especially when modeling domain results or UI states.

sections.heading: Advantages
items: Type Safety: Compiler enforces exhaustive handling in when expressions.
Readability: Makes UI state flow clear between ViewModel and UI layer.
Maintainability: Groups related states in a single structured hierarchy.

sections.heading: Summary
items: Sealed classes provide a closed inheritance hierarchy.
They allow stateful subclasses unlike enums.
Commonly used for Result types and UI states.
Work perfectly with when expressions for exhaustive checks.
